name: di-providing
title: DI provider scoping — root vs route-level vs component-level
description: Pattern for choosing where to provide an Angular service. Wrong choice causes either bloat (root for feature-specific service) or duplication (component for shared state). Pure pattern entry — no component source.

decisionTree.if: Service is used app-wide AND has no per-feature state
Service is used only within one lazy-loaded feature AND should be torn down when leaving the feature
decisionTree.use: Route-level providers via providers: [...] on the route
decisionTree.example: MessagingFeatureService (lives while user is in messaging)

decisionTree.if: Service holds per-instance state that should NOT be shared across instances of the same component
decisionTree.use: Component-level providers via providers: [...] on @Component decorator

gotchas: providedIn: 'root' is the right default — only deviate when you have a specific reason (lifecycle scope or per-instance state)
Route-level providers require a routing entry that has both `providers: [...]` AND a child loadComponent (or a children array). The providers belong to the route, not the leaf component.
Component-level providers create a new injector for the component AND its children — this affects every consumer down the tree, not just the component itself.

preferenceLedgerRows.name: functional DI via inject()
preferenceLedgerRows.applies: consumers use inject(Service) regardless of provider scope
